question: Explain why bus arbitration is necessary in a computer system with multiple bus masters.
expectedPoints: Prevents data corruption and system errors by ensuring only one device controls the bus at a time.
Resolves contention when multiple masters (e.g., CPU, DMA controller, I/O processors) need simultaneous access.
Facilitates fair and efficient access to shared resources, preventing a single master from monopolizing the bus.

question: Compare and contrast centralized and distributed bus arbitration techniques, providing an example of each.
expectedPoints: Centralized arbitration uses a single bus controller/arbiter (e.g., in a daisy-chain configuration) to grant access.
Distributed arbitration has no central controller; each device decides priority itself (e.g., using a self-selection method).
Example of centralized: Daisy-chain arbitration, where the grant signal propagates serially.
Example of distributed: Parallel (or collision-detection) arbitration, where devices assert priority codes on the bus.

question: Describe the process of daisy-chain arbitration in detail, including its advantages and major disadvantage.
expectedPoints: Process: A single Bus Request (BR) line is shared by all devices. The arbiter asserts a Bus Grant (BG) signal that propagates serially from the highest-priority device to the lowest.
The first device that has a request pending asserts the Bus Busy (BB) line and stops the propagation of BG, gaining control.
Advantage: Simple and inexpensive, requiring few control lines (BR, BG, BB).
Major Disadvantage: Fixed, inflexible priority; a low-priority device may be starved if high-priority devices make frequent requests.
Another disadvantage: The serial propagation adds latency, which becomes worse with more devices on the chain.
